title: MegaCorp Infrastructure Ltd Files for Bankruptcy Under IBC; Rs 47,000 Crore Debt at Risk
content:
MegaCorp Infrastructure Limited, one of India's largest infrastructure conglomerates with projects spanning highways, metro rail, and commercial real estate across 12 states, has filed for bankruptcy under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) at the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT), Mumbai bench.

The company owes approximately Rs 47,000 crore to a consortium of 14 banks led by State Bank of India. The filing comes after MegaCorp failed to meet debt repayment obligations for three consecutive quarters, despite multiple restructuring attempts.

Key creditors and their exposure:
- State Bank of India: Rs 12,000 crore
- Bank of Baroda: Rs 8,500 crore
- Punjab National Bank: Rs 6,200 crore
- ICICI Bank: Rs 5,800 crore
- Remaining 10 banks: Rs 14,500 crore combined

The filing lists total assets of Rs 31,000 crore against total liabilities of Rs 52,000 crore, revealing a negative net worth of Rs 21,000 crore. MegaCorp's stock, which was already under SEBI's Additional Surveillance Measure (ASM) framework since January 2026, has been suspended from trading on both BSE and NSE effective immediately.

The company employs approximately 34,000 workers across 12 states, with ongoing projects worth Rs 28,000 crore now at risk of significant delays. The National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) has confirmed that 6 highway projects awarded to MegaCorp are currently under various stages of construction.

Retired Justice K.V. Ramanathan has been appointed as the Resolution Professional. The Committee of Creditors (CoC) is expected to hold its first meeting within 14 days.

MegaCorp's promoter, Vikram Deshmukh, who holds a 28.4% stake in the company, had attempted a one-time settlement offer of Rs 18,000 crore last month, which was rejected by the banking consortium as inadequate. The company's auditors, Sharma & Associates, had flagged going concern issues in the last two quarterly reports.

Industry analysts note that MegaCorp's collapse is the largest infrastructure bankruptcy since IL&FS in 2018, and could trigger a review of lending norms for infrastructure projects. Banking stocks fell 1.2-2.8% in early afternoon trading following the announcement.
author: ET Bureau
